Title: Ascorbic acid-mediated reactive oxygen species homeostasis modulates the switch from tapetal cell division to cell differentiation in Arabidopsis

Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Anther wall cells including epidermis, endothecium, and tapetum; later signal around microspores/locule in reporter lines
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant/redox buffer that modulates ROS homeostasis and tapetal differentiation
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transcripts show no obvious early difference between WT and tdf1; VTC1-GFP accumulates in epidermis and endothecium and in tapetum during stages 5–9, with elevated accumulation in tdf1 tapetum
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher VTC1 accumulation/overexpression raises AsA, lowers tapetal H2O2, induces supernumerary tapetal cell divisions and perturbs male fertility; vtc1-3 suppresses tdf1 tapetal defects
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Tapetum-specific overexpression; translational reporter analysis; immunoblotting; AsA quantification; double-mutant analysis with tdf1; histology/FM4-64 staining
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overexpression causes partial multilayered tapetum, compromised pollen viability/male fertility; deficiency lowers AsA
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper treats VTC1 primarily as a TDF1-controlled AsA biosynthetic determinant; TDF1 affects VTC1 protein accumulation rather than transcript abundance
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VTC1 overexpression in tapetum increases AsA content. 2. Elevated VTC1 causes extra tapetal cell divisions and reduced pollen viability. 3. VTC1 protein accumulation is elevated in tdf1 despite little transcript change. 4. The vtc1-3 tdf1 double mutant abolishes the defective tapetal phenotype of tdf1, indicating VTC1-mediated AsA biosynthesis contributes to the defect.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: VTC1 is a strong leverage point for modulating AsA content, but reproductive-tissue overexpression disrupts tapetum development and fertility, so tissue and dosage control are essential
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: TDF1 (regulatory relationship); CSN5B discussed as possible interactor from prior work
Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: DYT1550 promoter for overexpression; native VTC1 promoter for reporter

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: AsA serves as antioxidant substrate in ROS scavenging; altered ROS affects tapetal cell division/differentiation
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detected mainly in tapetum in DYT1550pro:APX1-GFP lines at stages 4–7
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Tapetum-specific overexpression scavenges H2O2/lowers ROS and causes multilayered tapetum and severe pollen abortion
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Tapetum-specific overexpression, GFP localization, RT-qPCR, histology, Alexander staining
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as functional evidence that low ROS is sufficient to trigger excess tapetal cell divisions downstream of AsA homeostasis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. APX1 overexpression in tapetum causes male sterility/pollen abortion. 2. APX1-GFP accumulates mainly in tapetum. 3. Lower ROS through APX1 overexpression induces multilayered tapetum, supporting ROS as the downstream effector of AsA.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful as a tool to manipulate reproductive-tissue ROS, but not suitable for fertility-preserving engineering due to strong male-sterile phenotype

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: AsA-mediated redox system affects ROS homeostasis that controls tapetal differentiation
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detected mainly in tapetum in DYT1550pro:CAT3-GFP lines at stages 5–8
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Tapetum-specific overexpression scavenges H2O2/lowers ROS and causes multilayered tapetum with compromised male fertility
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Tapetum-specific overexpression, GFP localization, RT-qPCR, histology, Alexander staining
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Provides independent evidence that reduced ROS downstream of elevated AsA is sufficient to increase tapetal mitotic activity
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CAT3 overexpression in tapetum compromises male fertility. 2. CAT3-GFP accumulates mainly in tapetum. 3. Lowering ROS via CAT3 overexpression induces multilayered tapetum similar to tdf1/sks18/VTC1-OE.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Can be used to manipulate tapetal ROS experimentally, but overexpression is detrimental to pollen development

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: required for oxidation/depletion of target product
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes L-ascorbic acid (AsA) to oxidized ascorbate forms with concomitant reduction of oxygen to water; enzyme activity requires copper cofactor
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major antioxidant/redox buffer that controls ROS homeostasis; appropriate AsA levels enable tapetal transition from cell division to differentiation and support pollen development
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Preferentially expressed in tapetum during anther stages 6–7, reduced after microspore release
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes AsA oxidation, lowers AsA accumulation and helps prevent supernumerary tapetal cell divisions; overexpression partially rescues the defective tapetum of tdf1
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA knockout; CRISPR mutant; genetic complementation; tapetum-specific overexpression; recombinant enzyme assay with CuSO4; AsA quantification; localization imaging; histology/FM4-64 staining
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Copper cofactor addition is required to detect in vitro ascorbate oxidase activity
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss causes extra tapetal cells and mild pollen abortion; strong overexpression lowers AsA, raises H2O2, enlarges tapetal cells and reduces tapetal cell number
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mutation partially phenocopies tdf1, indicating SKS18 is a major but not sole TDF1 effector; classified previously as monocopper oxidase-like protein
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SKS18 is specifically expressed in differentiating tapetum and is abolished in tdf1. 2. SKS18 protein localizes to the apoplast/cell wall of tapetal cells. 3. Recombinant SKS18 exhibits copper-dependent ascorbate oxidase activity. 4. sks18 mutants accumulate more AsA and show extra tapetal cell divisions. 5. SKS18 overexpression in tdf1 lowers AsA and partially mitigates defective tapetum.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating SKS18 offers a route to tune AsA–ROS balance in reproductive tissues; useful for engineering male fertility/tapetum development, but dosage must be controlled because excessive expression suppresses tapetal proliferation and perturbs fertility
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Microarray candidate target dataset from prior study referenced; accession NA

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant/redox buffer controlling ROS homeostasis and the switch from tapetal cell proliferation to differentiation, thereby supporting pollen development
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: AsA homeostasis through coordinating oxidation and biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Direct transcriptional activation of SKS18 and negative control of VTC1 protein accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates SKS18 transcription directly; negatively controls VTC1 accumulation post-transcriptionally or indirectly
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Central regulator that lowers AsA content to maintain proper ROS levels and prevent excess tapetal division
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduces AsA accumulation and promotes tapetal differentiation by constraining supernumerary cell divisions; loss of function raises AsA and lowers tapetal H2O2
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant phenotyping; ChIP-qPCR; EMSA; transient dual-luciferase assay; reporter/localization analyses; genetic rescue by SKS18 overexpression and vtc1-3 tdf1 double mutant; AsA and ROS measurements
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Can form/additively function with AMS for some tapetal genes, but SKS18 activation is TDF1-specific and not significantly enhanced by AMS
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss causes multilayered hypertrophic defective tapetum, male sterility, altered ROS distribution and failed tapetum differentiation
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts as a switch triggering exit from tapetal cell division and entry into differentiation; also previously reported to activate other tapetal genes such as AMS and EXPB5
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TDF1 inhibits tapetal cell divisions and promotes secretory differentiation. 2. TDF1 directly binds the SKS18 promoter and activates SKS18 expression. 3. TDF1 negatively regulates AsA accumulation through SKS18-mediated oxidation and by restraining VTC1 accumulation. 4. Loss of TDF1 elevates AsA, lowers tapetal H2O2, and causes supernumerary tapetal cell divisions. 5. Lowering AsA genetically partially rescues the tdf1 tapetal defect.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: TDF1 is a master reproductive regulator for engineering redox-controlled tapetum development and male fertility, but manipulation likely carries strong fertility penalties
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: 35S in transient activation assay; native TDF1 promoter in complementation/reporter constructs
